skills.name: azure-lab
skills.description: Build ephemeral Azure lab environments to test Azure features. Designs test scenarios, deploys Azure resources (including ExpressRoute with Megaport MCR integration), captures CLI output and portal screenshots, collects routing tables at multiple layers, and generates a comprehensive report. Cleans up all resources after testing. Use this skill when the user asks to test an Azure feature, build an Azure lab, investigate Azure networking behavior, or verify Azure service configurations.

skills.name: docx
skills.description: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.

skills.name: excalidraw
skills.description: Generate Excalidraw diagrams from descriptions. Creates .excalidraw JSON files that can be opened in https://aka.ms/excalidraw or embedded in documentation. Use when creating architecture diagrams, flowcharts, data flow visualizations, or illustrations for proposals and design documents.

skills.name: expense-report
skills.description: Create and fill out Microsoft Dynamics 365 MyExpense expense reports from receipts using visible browser automation. Handles travel expenses, recurring monthly expenses (internet, phone), hotel itemization, and corporate AMEX matching. Produces a complete, review-ready draft; the user always submits themselves. Triggers include: 'expense report', 'submit expense', 'file an expense', 'internet expense', 'monthly expense', 'travel expense', or any request to log a reimbursable cost.

skills.name: loop
skills.description: Edit Microsoft Loop documents in the browser using Playwright automation. Use when the user wants to create, edit, or update a Loop document, mentions 'Loop', 'Loop page', or 'Loop workspace', or wants to add content to a shared document in Loop.

skills.name: pptx
skills.description: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments; working with SharePoint-hosted or OneDrive-hosted presentations (even when no local file is involved). Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename or a SharePoint/OneDrive URL pointing to a presentation, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill. IMPORTANT: If the input is a SharePoint or OneDrive URL, read ~/.copilot/skills/pptx/sharepoint.md before doing anything else.

skills.name: web-artifacts-builder
skills.description: Create interactive HTML artifacts (dashboards, visualizations, org charts, network maps, trackers, comparisons) as self-contained HTML files. Use whenever the user requests a web application, data visualization, interactive tool, or any HTML artifact. All output MUST use the Clawpilot theme variables defined in this skill.

skills.name: xlsx
skills.description: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.

skills.name: customize-cloud-agent
skills.description: Skill for customizing the Copilot cloud agent (formerly known as Copilot coding agent) environment, including copilot-setup-steps.yml configuration, preinstalling tools and dependencies, runners, and settings. Use when the user mentions copilot-setup-steps, copilot setup steps, or wants to configure the cloud agent environment.

skills.name: calendar

Triggers include "calendar", "meeting", "schedule", "event", "invite", "availability", "free/busy", "meeting room".

skills.name: deep-research
skills.description: Conduct enterprise-grade research using WorkIQ to search internal M365 data via Microsoft Graph. Use when user needs comprehensive analysis of enterprise data (emails, meetings, Teams chats, files, wikis), verified claims, or synthesis across internal sources. Also use for generating grounded executive status reports for the your program. Triggers include "deep research", "comprehensive analysis", "research report", "executive status report", "program status", "generate exec report", or any request requiring synthesis of internal enterprise data.

skills.name: cocoindex
skills.description: Comprehensive toolkit for developing with the CocoIndex library. Use when users need to create data transformation pipelines (flows), write custom functions, or operate flows via CLI or API. Covers building ETL workflows for AI data processing, including embedding documents into vector databases, building knowledge graphs, creating search indexes, or processing data streams with incremental updates. Triggers include "cocoindex", "data pipeline", "indexing flow", "embedding pipeline", "vector index", "knowledge graph", "ETL", "data transformation".

skills.name: confluence
skills.description: Browse, search, create, and edit Confluence wiki pages. Connects to a Confluence Server instance via Azure AD SAML SSO.

skills.name: deep-personalization
skills.description: Use this skill when the user asks to "personalize my agent", "set up my coworker", "configure my identity", "customize my workspace", "deep personalization", "interview me", "get to know me", "build my profile", "set up my preferences", "onboard me", or any request to personalize the agent's identity, communication style, domain knowledge, or working preferences. This is a guided multi-phase interview that writes to CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and memory/MEMORY.md.

skills.name: email-triage
skills.description:
Use this skill when the user asks to "triage my email", "check my inbox",
"process my emails", "what needs my attention", "email triage", "categorize emails",
"sort my inbox", "filter my emails", "what's urgent in my inbox", "draft responses",
"inbox zero", or wants automated email categorization and response drafting.
Runs on a 30-minute schedule during working hours or on demand.
Powered by microsoft-outlook-mail MCP + microsoft-teams MCP.

skills.name: excel
skills.description:
Use this skill when the user asks to "edit a spreadsheet", "update an Excel file", "modify a workbook",
"create an xlsx", "inspect a spreadsheet", or wants to create, inspect, edit, or modify Excel workbooks.
Primary workflow is edit-first: download → inspect → batch edit → upload back.

skills.name: landing-zone
skills.description: Query, analyze, and manage Azure DevOps Landing Zone requirements. Supports syncing ADO saved queries, state machine validation, grading analytics, week-over-week progress tracking, and ADO write operations (create, assign, grade, reparent, link). Triggers include "landing zone", "LZ", "requirements", "grading status", "POR status", "domain status", "LZ sync", "at risk requirements", "ungraded items", "week over week", "WoW comparison".

skills.name: markitdown
skills.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "convert a document to markdown", "add a file to the knowledgebase", "convert PDF/Word/Excel/PowerPoint to markdown", or wants to convert any supported document format into a .md file for the Knowledgebase. Supports PDF, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, HTML, images, audio, CSV, JSON, XML, ZIP, YouTube URLs, and EPubs.

skills.name: meeting-summary
skills.description: Generate executive meeting summaries from Teams meeting recaps and transcripts. Use this skill when the user asks to "summarize a meeting", "create meeting notes", "get meeting recap", "write meeting summary", or wants to produce formatted meeting notes from a Teams meeting. Triggers include "meeting summary", "meeting notes", "recap", "summarize the meeting", "what happened in the meeting".

skills.name: onepdm
skills.description:
Search, browse, and download specifications from OnePDM (Aras Innovator PLM).
Use when the user asks to "download a spec", "search OnePDM", "find specification M1345662",
"list specs", "check spec freshness", "import SOW exhibits", "update specs", or any
operation involving OnePDM document retrieval. Triggers: "onepdm", "specification",
"spec download", "plm", "document number", "M1", "download spec", "list specs",
"check freshness", "import sow".

skills.name: oneplanner
skills.description: Manage Microsoft Project for the Web schedules via the OnePlanner local REST API

skills.name: powerpoint
skills.description:
Use this skill when the user asks to "create a PowerPoint", "make a deck", "build a presentation",
"edit slides", "update this pptx", "redesign this deck", "modify a presentation", "add slides",
"create a pptx for my meeting", or wants any PowerPoint creation, editing, inspection, or redesign.
Also triggers on "make me a slide deck about...", "I need a presentation for...", "convert this to slides".

skills.name: qmd-memory
skills.description: Use this skill for ALL access to the agent's memory system — both user-facing requests and internal agent operations. Triggers include: the agent needing to read, search, or write ANY file under the memory/ directory (daily logs, knowledgebase, MEMORY.md, weekly reports); user asks to "search my memory", "find past decisions", "look up knowledgebase content", "what do we know about <topic>", "recall <something>", "search notes for <topic>", "re-index memory", "update memory index"; user asks to "remember this" or "update my profile"; user says "goodbye", "end session", "that's all", "save context", or "flush memory". IMPORTANT: Do NOT grep, glob, or view files in memory/ directly — always go through this skill for search and retrieval. Direct file reads (view tool) are only acceptable when you already have the exact path from a prior skill search result.

skills.name: send-email
skills.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "send an email to someone", "email this to <person>", "send a message to <email>", "reply to an email", "forward this email", "draft an email", "search my emails", "find emails about <topic>", or wants to compose, send, reply, forward, search, or manage emails via Outlook. Unlike notify-me which sends to yourself, this skill sends to any specified recipient(s). Powered by the microsoft-outlook-mail MCP.

skills.name: sharepoint
skills.description: Use this skill when the user wants to download a file from SharePoint, retrieve a document from a SharePoint link, save a SharePoint file locally, add a SharePoint document to the Knowledgebase, upload a local file to SharePoint or OneDrive, or transfer files between local machine and cloud storage. Handles files of any size by downloading/uploading via the Microsoft Graph API, bypassing the 5 MB limit of the SharePoint MCP read/write tools. Can optionally convert documents to Markdown using markitdown.

skills.name: spec-kit
skills.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a spec", "write a specification", "plan a feature", "initialize spec-driven development", "generate a constitution", "break down tasks", "implement from spec", or wants to use spec-driven development to plan and build software systematically. Triggers include "spec-kit", "speckit", "specify", "spec-driven", "create a plan", "generate tasks from spec", or "implement the plan".

skills.name: svg-to-ppt
skills.description: Use this skill when the user asks to "create a diagram for PowerPoint", "make an SVG slide", "convert SVG to PPT", "build an architecture diagram", "generate editable PowerPoint shapes", or needs SVG diagrams that convert cleanly to editable PowerPoint shapes. Triggers include "svg", "diagram to ppt", "editable shapes", "architecture diagram", "process diagram".

skills.name: task-scheduler
skills.description: Use this skill when the user asks to "schedule a task", "create a recurring task", "run something on a schedule", "list scheduled tasks", "pause a task", "resume a task", "check task status", "view task logs", "start the scheduler", "stop the scheduler", or any task scheduling operation. This skill manages one-time and recurring tasks that invoke Agency Copilot with stored prompts, powered by a persistent background service.

skills.name: teams
skills.description: Use this skill when the user wants to interact with Microsoft Teams — send messages (plain or rich), read chats or channels, manage teams/channels/members, search conversations, send @mentions, Adaptive Cards, or file attachments. Also use when the user asks to "refresh Teams cache", "list my Teams chats", "send a Teams message", "send a formatted message", "mention someone", "send a card", "attach a file in Teams", or any Teams-related operation. This skill wraps the microsoft-teams MCP with local caching for fast lookups, and adds rich messaging via a Playwright browser session.

skills.name: visual-explainer
skills.description: Generate beautiful, self-contained HTML pages that visually explain systems, code changes, plans, and data. Use when the user asks for a diagram, architecture overview, diff review, plan review, project recap, comparison table, or any visual explanation of technical concepts. Also use proactively when you are about to render a complex ASCII table (4+ rows or 3+ columns) -- present it as a styled HTML page instead. Origin: github.com/nicobailon/visual-explainer (MIT, v0.6.2).

skills.name: webpage-builder
skills.description: Cinematic landing page builder. Generates high-fidelity React + GSAP + Tailwind sites from 4 questions. Invoke when the user asks to "build a landing page", "create a website", "make a site", or "webpage-builder".

skills.name: weekly-report
skills.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "generate a weekly report", "write weekly status", "create a weekly update", "draft exec bullets", or wants to produce a weekly status report for a program. Also triggers on "status update for executives", "weekly bullets", "program update", "executive status report", or "program status".

skills.name: word-doc
skills.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a Word document", "edit a Word file", "update a docx", "read a Word file", "inspect a document", "get document content", "comment on a document", "reply to a comment", or wants to create, read, edit, or collaborate on Microsoft Word documents. Supports both cloud (MCP/OneDrive) and local (python-docx) editing. Primary workflow is edit-first: download → inspect → batch edit → upload back.

skills.name: workstreams
skills.description:
Manage program workstreams — meeting summaries, action item tracking,
and Landing Zone cross-referencing. Use this skill when the user asks to
"summarize a workstream meeting", "track action items", "list workstreams",
"workstream status", "post to workstream channel", "what are the open actions",
"show overdue items", or any workstream management operation. Triggers include
"workstream", "action items", "meeting notes for <workstream>", "post summary to channel".

skills.name: account-acr
skills.description: Pull Azure Consumed Revenue for an account from C360 (canonical aggregate) and cross-validate with MSXI enrollment-grain detail. Surfaces MoM / QoQ / YoY trends and flags any cross-system mismatch. STU primary skill.

skills.name: account-brief
skills.description: Generate a structured one-page profile for a single customer account — top-parent TPID, segment, AM, agreements, MACC, ACR run-rate, support posture, churn signal. The ATU's daily orientation tool. Output is written to the Copilot CLI session workspace by default; an optional personal markdown vault can be enabled via `vault_path` or the `AI_SALES_KIT_VAULT_PATH` env var (see `references/personal-vault-integration.md`).

skills.name: account-delivery-status
skills.description: Surface the sale-to-delivery seam for a single account — Customer Success Plan coverage of milestones, engagement milestones with $ value from ESXP, gaps between what MSX shows as Won and what ESXP shows as a live delivery. CSU primary skill, ATU / STU read-along when assessing handoff hygiene.

skills.name: account-macc
skills.description: Pull MACC commitment status for an account — committed, drawn down, balance, runway, expiration, at-risk flags. Native sales tool returns server-computed runway analysis. CSU primary skill.

skills.name: account-motion-alignment
skills.description: Compare an account's active pipeline (milestone $/mo per pillar) against its actual Azure consumption (FY ACR share per pillar). Surfaces over-indexed pipeline (good — future motion exists), under-indexed pipeline (dark territory — large ACR with no active engagement), and "off-pillar" pipeline. STU/ATU joint skill.

skills.name: account-pipeline
skills.description: Pull open opportunities, engagement milestones, and Pipe WoW changes for a single account. Joins Dynamics CRM (live opps + milestones) with MSXI Pipe WoW Summary to surface what moved this week. ATU primary skill.

skills.name: account-support-coverage
skills.description: Pull support and delivery coverage signals for an account — open Sev A/B tickets, Unified Delivery Scrum status, VBD Insights workload posture. CSU secondary skill (covers what was originally "support attach").

skills.name: account-team-radar
skills.description: Universal. Given an account (TPID or name), list every Microsoft seller currently working open opportunities — across all teams. Useful for cross-team coordination, account-handoff context, or "who else is touching this customer".

skills.name: account-workload-adoption
skills.description: Pull workload-specific adoption signals for an account. Covers Azure / M365 Copilot / Security / BizApps / Support — pick the workload via the --workload parameter. STU secondary skill.

skills.name: attainment
skills.description: Pull the seller's MSX Earnings (MINT) attainment summary — total weighted attainment, per-PCG bucket details, payment partition status, and active notifications. Universal — works for ATU/STU/CSU.

skills.name: kit-bootstrap
skills.description: Deterministic new-user setup. Runs the full az / Edge / tab / preflight / cache-prime chain in a fixed order and emits a typed informational envelope per slice (ready / not-ready + fix hint). The canonical entry point for first-time users and a clean re-attach after an Edge crash or sign-out. New in v0.25.1 (F6).

skills.name: kit-warmup
skills.description: Prime the MSXI direct-QES plan cache deterministically so the first real seller question of the session pays warm-cache latency, not cold-start. Runs after Edge is attached and signed in. New in v0.25.1 (F1).

skills.name: mcem-coach
skills.description: Deep MCEM coaching on a specific opportunity or milestone. Identifies the current stage, lists Verifiable Outcomes for the next gate, surfaces missing evidence, names the receiving role for the next handoff seam, and flags anti-patterns. Universal — works for ATU/STU/CSU.

skills.name: msxi-reverse-engineer
skills.description: Reverse-engineer an MSXI Power BI report into typed direct-QES MCP tools. Captures the embed's QES traffic, conceptual schema, and visual configs; produces a per-report RE devdoc and the artifacts needed to ship a new `msxi_<report>_<visual>` tool family. Use when the seller wants native plugin coverage for a report not yet wired up — or when running the multi-persona sweep to collect captures from a seller whose MSXI profile has access the plugin maintainer doesn't.

skills.name: my-milestones
skills.description: List the seller's open Dynamics CRM milestones — matches the C360 "My Milestones" web view exactly. Single MCP call via list_my_milestones (which handles the systemuserid lookup, the canonical filter, and OData flattening internally).

skills.name: my-open-opportunities
skills.description: List the seller's open Dynamics CRM opportunities — the equivalent of the "a) My Open Opportunities" view in C360. Resolves the seller's CRM systemuserid first, filters by _ownerid_value, and renders Opportunity Id, Topic, Account, Stage, Est. Close Date.

skills.name: onboard-seller
skills.description: One-shot persona discovery for a new ai-sales-kit user. Walks the preflight + identity + team-membership + portfolio chain in a defined order, then writes a confirmed persona summary so subsequent skills can skip the cold-start dance.

skills.name: plugin-feedback
skills.description: Capture structured user feedback on this plugin (ai-sales-kit) for the engineering team. Produces a single Markdown report naming the MCPs / skills invoked, the friction the user hit, the reframes they had to make, the workflow patterns they fell into, and concrete suggestions the engineering team can turn into backlog items. Anchored on the session_store_sql cross-session DuckDB (sessions/turns/events/tool_requests). Triggers include "give feedback on this plugin", "feedback for ai-sales-kit", "what hurt me in this session", "report friction", "plugin feedback", "log my friction with the kit", "send feedback to engineering", "post-mortem this session for the engineering team". Use after a real working session when the friction is fresh.

skills.name: prep-1on1
skills.description: Manager-only. Generate a 1:1-prep brief for a specific direct report — their book at a glance, top deals, what's at risk, recent movement, and suggested coaching topics. Plain-language coaching tone.

skills.name: setup-edge
skills.description: Launch Microsoft Edge with the Chrome DevTools Protocol enabled, open MSX/MSXI/CRM/ESXP tabs, and verify all three local MCPs can capture tokens. Run this once at the start of each session.

skills.name: team-attainment
skills.description: Manager-only. Pulls every direct report's MSX Earnings (MINT) attainment summary, sorted by who's furthest behind. Surfaces team-wide totalWeightedAttainment, per-bucket gaps, and active notifications. Pre-forecast and pre-1:1 prep.

skills.name: team-forecast-quality
skills.description: Manager-only. Pulls Commit + Best Case opportunities across the team and flags forecast-quality gaps — missing Champion, missing compelling event, stalled stage velocity, no recent activity. The pre-forecast-call ammunition skill for sales managers like SSP / DES leads.

skills.name: team-handoff-hygiene
skills.description: Manager-only. Surfaces handoff-hygiene gaps across the team — Stage 4 Commit deals without CSAM on the opportunity team, Stage 4-5 Uncommitted milestones still open, missing Customer Success Plan signals. The MCEM-aware "what's about to fall through the cracks" view.

skills.name: team-pipeline-health
skills.description: Manager-only. Snapshot of the whole team's pipeline — milestones + open opps across all direct reports. Surfaces stage distribution per report, At Risk / Blocked counts, $ at risk, top stalled deals, and stale-pipeline % per seller. The Monday-morning manager dashboard.

skills.name: upgrade-ai-sales-kit
skills.description: Pull the latest ai-sales-kit, rebuild, and reinstall the Copilot plugin. Handles the stale-MCP-process lock that normally blocks `copilot plugin install` mid-session. Run this when the user says "upgrade the plugin", "update ai-sales-kit", "pull the latest", or after you ship a fix and want them on it.

skills.name: verify-schema
skills.description: Compare the bundled msxi-mcp report catalog against the live MSXI window.userReportsData.data and report any drift (added, removed, renamed, audience-changed, GUID-changed). Run periodically to detect when the plugin needs a refresh.

skills.name: customize-cloud-agent
skills.description: Skill for customizing the Copilot cloud agent (formerly known as Copilot coding agent) environment, including copilot-setup-steps.yml configuration, preinstalling tools and dependencies, runners, and settings. Use when the user mentions copilot-setup-steps, copilot setup steps, or wants to configure the cloud agent environment.

skills.name: diff-review
skills.description: Generate a visual HTML diff review — before/after architecture comparison with code review analysis

skills.name: fact-check
skills.description: Verify the factual accuracy of a document against the actual codebase, correct inaccuracies in place

skills.name: generate-slides
skills.description: Generate a stunning magazine-quality slide deck as a self-contained HTML page

skills.name: generate-visual-plan
skills.description: Generate a visual HTML implementation plan — detailed feature specification with state machines, code snippets, and edge cases

skills.name: generate-web-diagram
skills.description: Generate a beautiful standalone HTML diagram and open it in the browser

skills.name: plan-review
skills.description: Generate a visual HTML plan review — current codebase state vs. proposed implementation plan

skills.name: project-recap
skills.description: Generate a visual HTML project recap — rebuild mental model of a project's current state, recent decisions, and cognitive debt hotspots

skills.name: share
skills.description: Share a visual explainer HTML file instantly via Vercel. Returns a live URL with no authentication required. ``` /share <file-path>

data.content:
You are a strict, terse judge for cloud-networking answers.

Question:
running ~500 Mbps of steady on-prem-to-Azure traffic; help me decide between ExpressRoute and VPN gateway on a TCO basis

Expected topic specialists (for context): cn_price,cn_hyb

--- Answer A ---
**TCO call:** for ~500 Mbps steady **on-prem → Azure only**, a **VPN Gateway is usually cheaper** on Azure charges because inbound data to Azure is generally not billed; ExpressRoute adds fixed circuit/gateway/provider costs that may not be offset by data-transfer savings.

| Factor | VPN Gateway | ExpressRoute |
|---|---:|---:|
| Best-fit SKU | VpnGw3/VpnGw4+ for headroom | 1 Gbps circuit minimum practical fit |
| Cost shape | Low fixed monthly gateway cost | Higher fixed Azure + carrier/provider MRC |
| Data cost for on-prem → Azure | Typically $0 Azure ingress | Typically $0 Azure ingress |
| Performance risk | Internet jitter, IPsec overhead, variable throughput | Predictable latency/throughput, private path |
| SLA/operations | Good, but depends on internet + VPN devices | Better for production steady-state hybrid traffic |

**Recommendation:** choose **VPN Gateway** if this is mostly one-way ingestion, moderate SLA, and cost is the main driver. Choose **ExpressRoute** if the workload is business-critical, latency-sensitive, needs predictable 500 Mbps sustained throughput, has compliance/private-path requirements, or has meaningful **Azure → on-prem** return traffic where ExpressRoute billing model may become more favorable.

For TCO, price these three scenarios: **VpnGw3/4 active-active**, **1 Gbps ExpressRoute metered**, and **1 Gbps ExpressRoute unlimited**, including carrier cross-connect/MPLS/ISP costs. Verify current prices in Azure Pricing Calculator and your connectivity provider quote before committing.

--- Answer B ---
I’ll route this through the loaded network-desk specialists, then give a concise TCO-focused recommendation.

**Recommendation: choose ExpressRoute for steady ~500 Mbps production traffic; keep VPN Gateway as backup.**

500 Mbps steady is ~**164 TB/month**. Even if most traffic is **on-prem → Azure** and Azure ingress is typically not charged, VPN is operating near practical limits: a 650 Mbps VPN SKU has little headroom after IPsec overhead, and a 1 Gbps+ VPN SKU still uses best-effort internet. For TCO, ExpressRoute costs more in fixed monthly fees, but buys predictable latency, SLA-backed private connectivity, cleaner operations, and better economics if any meaningful Azure → on-prem egress exists.

| Option | Likely monthly Azure-side cost drivers | TCO fit at 500 Mbps steady |
|---|---:|---|
| **VPN Gateway** | VpnGw2AZ/VpnGw3AZ roughly **$500–$1,000/mo** + bandwidth egress if Azure sends data out | Cheapest fixed cost, but high utilization, internet variability, IPsec overhead, weaker fit for steady production load |
| **ExpressRoute 1 Gbps** | Circuit around **$1,100/mo** + ER gateway roughly **$139–$781/mo** + provider/cross-connect fees + metered/unlimited data plan | Better fit: sized at ~50% utilization, predictable performance, often lower data-transfer cost for Azure → on-prem flows |
| **ExpressRoute 500 Mbps** | Lower circuit fee, but 500 Mbps steady leaves no growth/failover headroom | Avoid unless traffic is capped and non-critical |

**TCO decision rule:** if the 500 Mbps is business-critical, latency-sensitive, expected to grow, or includes substantial Azure → on-prem return traffic, ExpressRoute is the better TCO choice. If it is non-critical, short-lived, mostly ingress-only, and you can tolerate internet-path variability, VPN Gateway is the lower-cost option.

Use current Azure pricing pages to confirm regional rates: https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/expressroute/, https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/vpn-gateway/, and https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/bandwidth/.

Analysis only — verify against vendor documentation before applying.

Score each answer independently on a 0-10 scale (0=useless, 10=excellent) for:
  technical_accuracy, completeness, actionability, clarity, conciseness

Then pick the winner. Penalize hallucinated vendor flags, wrong CLI syntax,
or guidance that contradicts the platform documentation. Reward concrete,
verifiable detail; do not reward verbosity.

Return ONE single-line JSON object, NO markdown, NO commentary, exactly:
{"A":{"technical_accuracy":N,"completeness":N,"actionability":N,"clarity":N,"conciseness":N},"B":{"technical_accuracy":N,"completeness":N,"actionability":N,"clarity":N,"conciseness":N},"winner":"A"|"B"|"tie","reason":"<=160 chars"}

data.content: {"A":{"technical_accuracy":7,"completeness":7,"actionability":7,"clarity":8,"conciseness":8},"B":{"technical_accuracy":7,"completeness":8,"actionability":8,"clarity":7,"conciseness":6},"winner":"B","reason":"B provides concrete cost ranges, data-volume math, and pricing links that make TCO comparison more actionable despite extra verbosity"}
data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to judge two answers about ExpressRoute vs VPN Gateway TCO for ~500 Mbps steady on-prem-to-Azure traffic. I need to score each on technical_accuracy, completeness, actionability, clarity, conciseness, then pick a winner.

Let me analyze both:

**Answer A:**
- Technical accuracy: Generally correct. VPN Gateway is cheaper for ingress-only. Correctly notes ingress is free for both. Correctly identifies performance tradeoffs. However, saying "VPN Gateway is usually cheaper" as the headline recommendation could be misleading for 500 Mbps steady traffic - that's a LOT of sustained throughput for a VPN. 
- VpnGw3 supports ~1.25 Gbps aggregate but with IPsec overhead, 500 Mbps steady is pushing it. The answer does acknowledge this risk. Score: 7
- Completeness: Covers key factors, mentions both directions of traffic, mentions SLA, mentions checking pricing calculator. Doesn't give specific dollar figures. Score: 7
- Actionability: Tells user to price three scenarios, check pricing calculator. Good but somewhat generic. Score: 7
- Clarity: Well-structured table, clear recommendation with conditions. Score: 8
- Conciseness: Reasonably concise, good use of table. Score: 8

Now checking Answer B's technical accuracy. The data transfer calculation for 500 Mbps over a month works out to roughly 162 TB, which is correct. The pricing estimates are in the ballpark—VpnGw2AZ around $548/month, VpnGw3AZ around $1,096/month, and ExpressRoute options varying depending on the circuit type and peering location.

Answer B scores well on completeness with specific cost ranges and all three ExpressRoute options covered, plus pricing links. It's actionable with clear decision criteria and well-organized, though the preamble about "network-desk specialists" adds some unnecessary verbosity. The recommendation to use ExpressRoute for steady production traffic at 500 Mbps is sound from a reliability perspective.

Now comparing the two answers—Answer A opens with the claim that VPN Gateway is usually cheaper, which is technically accurate for lower bandwidth scenarios, but potentially misleading when dealing with steady 500 Mbps production workloads. Answer B leads with ExpressRoute as the recommendation, which is more operationally appropriate for that use case, though it does qualify the answer appropriately. Both recognize that the decision hinges on traffic direction, criticality, and SLA requirements. Answer B's advantage lies in its specific cost figures and data volume calculations, making it more actionable. For steady production traffic, ExpressRoute is the stronger choice, though Answer A's more balanced "it depends" framing is arguably more accurate for scenarios with lower SLA requirements or pure ingress patterns. The real differentiator comes down to whether the specific numbers and production-focused recommendation in Answer B outweigh Answer A's conciseness and balanced perspective. The regional variation in those pricing estimates makes Answer B's numbers reasonable but not definitive, whereas Answer A's safer approach lacks the concrete detail needed for actual planning. I'm settling on B as the winner since the specific cost ranges and calculation examples give someone real numbers to work with for their TCO analysis, even if the verbosity costs it some points on conciseness.
